headline: SUHAKAM-Linked 2022 Estimates: 500,000 to 1 Million Stateless Children in Sabah
context: SUHAKAM-linked reporting in 2022 said there is no official figure for stateless children in Sabah and estimated 500,000 to 1 million. Tens of thousands are excluded from formal schooling per UNICEF. The Bajau Laut community is a highly visible affected group.

cards.big: SUHAKAM-linked 2022 reporting estimates 500,000 to 1 million stateless children in Sabah. There is no official figure.
cards.sub: Tens of thousands are excluded from formal schooling per UNICEF. Sabah has a very large population of non-citizens; the scale of statelessness within it is contested.

cards.big: Federal Constitution Article 14 grants citizenship by birth under specific conditions. Stateless children fall through gaps in jus soli and jus sanguinis.
cards.sub: The conditions assume parental documentation many lack, perpetuating statelessness across generations.

cards.big: Malaysia is not bound by the 1961 Statelessness Convention. It is a party to the CRC with reservations affecting Article 7 (right to acquire nationality).
cards.sub: Civil society has urged statelessness-reduction action. Successive governments have not enacted comprehensive citizenship-pathway legislation.

cards.big: The Bajau Laut are a frequently cited affected group, described by researchers as 'genuinely stateless' across Malaysia, Indonesia and the Philippines.
cards.sub: Many Bajau Laut have lived along Sabah's coast for generations; no state recognises them as citizens.

cards.big: UNICEF's Sabah study found 18,781 'invisible' children missing from official databases. SUHAKAM-linked reporting estimates the latent stateless population at 500K-1M.
Statelessness in Sabah is structural exclusion. It needs citizenship-pathway legislation and the will to count the affected population honestly.
